title: Five Intriguing Factors about the HIPAA Act and its effective Implementation at your Practice
excerpt: From HIPAA's five titles to monetary penalties and risk management strategies — here are five essential factors every practice must understand to stay compliant and avoid violations.
author: Seyyone Team
content:
The Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act, or HIPAA Act, was enacted on August 21, 1996. This Act had the Secretary of Health and Human Services (HHS) standardize electronic exchange, privacy, and security of Protected Health Information (PHI).

Today the majority of physicians and practices are slowly adapting to digitalization with a gradual ascent of EHR/EMR, patient inquiry portals, and insurance website portals. As there is a high concern for privacy and security policies, stringent enforcement by the HIPAA and the HITECH Act has become necessary.

It was in 2003 that The Department of Health and Human Services' Office for Civil Rights (OCR) was made responsible for HIPAA enforcement. There are five titles of the HIPAA Act governing PHI, prevention of healthcare fraud and abuse, administrative simplification, healthcare coverage modifications, medical savings accounts, and provisions on interest allocation rules.

  1. HIPAA and its Main Functions

    The most significant responsibilities that come with HIPAA are ensuring the integrity, confidentiality, and availability of healthcare information while providing access to Healthcare Providers, Payers, and Clearinghouses. Congress passed the HIPAA law to establish Federal standards for the security and privacy of protected health information.

    With the enforcement of the HITECH Act on February 17, 2009, even business associates such as billing companies and sub-contractors are subjected to comply with HIPAA.

  2. 5 Rules for Enforcing Administrative Simplification

    There are five rules for administrative simplification:

    • Privacy Rule: Regulates the usage and disclosure of PHI by covered entities. Patients should be well informed of how their personal data would be used or disclosed. The HITECH Act was implemented to promote and expand the adoption of Information Technology.
    • Transactions and Code Sets Rule: Intended for standardizing transactions in healthcare. Every provider who bills claims electronically after July 1, 2005, must engage in a HIPAA standardized way to get reimbursed.
    • Security Rule: A complement to the Privacy Rule, focusing on Electronic PHI. It has three security safeguards — Administrative (privacy policies and procedures), Physical (controlling physical access to PHI), and Technical (restricting access to unauthorized data manipulation).
    • Unique Identifiers Rule: Implies a standard 10-digit National Provider Identifier (NPI) which replaces any other identifiers assigned by Federal and Commercial Insurance Payers. As of 2007, only the NPI shall be used by covered entities.
    • Enforcement Rule: Establishes procedures to penalize the violation of HIPAA rules. Entities are subjected to take corrective measures when there is a breach.

  3. The Information that the HIPAA Act Protects

    All protected information falls under categories such as patient demographics, medical history, transaction history, and any provision on individual healthcare carrier which could reveal the identity of a patient. There should be limitations on the usage of PHI to a need to know basis. Access to potential healthcare data should be authorized or restricted based on how associates are adequately trained and licensed.

  4. HIPAA Violations and Monetary Penalties

    Monetary penalties are based on the level of negligence, separated by four tiers of culpability under the HITECH Act:

    • The person was not aware of, and by exercising reasonable diligence, would not have known they violated HIPAA
    • The violation occurred because of reasonable cause and not by willful neglect
    • The violation was due to willful neglect that is timely corrected
    • The violation was due to willful neglect that is not timely corrected

    These fines are usually issued by State Attorney General and the OCR. They range from $100 to $1,500,000 per each tier of violation per year.

  5. Implementation of Effective Risk Management Strategies

    A risk analysis must be done by reviewing similar Medical Groups. Common risks a practice may experience occur while storing, accessing, or transmitting PHI.

    • Accessing PHI: Ensure verified training and address unauthorized access issues. Install firewall and virus protection software on all portable devices. Enable two-factor authentication for remote access, and set default timeouts on inactive devices.
    • Storing PHI: Train associates on policies for deleted files. Prevent downloading EPHI onto remote devices without operational justification. Maintain a backup of data before deletion, always encrypted. Install biometric password protection on portable devices.
    • Transmitting PHI: Prohibit transmission of EPHI via open networks. SSL should be the minimum requirement for systems that manage EPHI. Implement message-level standards such as S/MIME, SET, PEM, PGP for email.

    These new standards and technologies have simplified data transmission but have also created complications and increased the risk of loss. These issues are easily manageable when the practice is partnered with a HIPAA compliant business associate.

title: Electronic medical records and patients privacy
excerpt: As healthcare organizations worldwide adopt EMR systems, critical questions arise around patient privacy, data security, and ethical responsibilities. Here is what every practice needs to know.
author: Seyyone Team
content:
Today most of the healthcare organizations all over the world are implementing electronic healthcare medical record systems. Even though with the emerging technologies, there is a need to upgrade to these systems, there are many complexities which need to be understood in these systems.

It is very important to understand the healthcare information system which is currently in use and what is the necessity of changing it to an electronic medical record system before even deciding to implement the same. A medical record — whether electronic or paper based — is a communication tool which supports coordination of services, clinical decision making, evaluating efficacy and quality of services, legal protection, research, accreditation, education, and regulatory processes.

The medical record is the business record of the health care system. In the past, and even today in some healthcare organizations, medical records are recorded as a paper repository. They are reviewed and used for research, clinical, financial, and administrative purposes.

When the record is on paper, it is highly limited in terms of availability and accessibility to a single user. These paper-based records must be updated manually, causing delays of 6 months or even more. Along with this, since there is a need for too much space for these records, healthcare organizations place them in basements.

There is one more limitation for this paper-based system — lack of security. Even though access is controlled by locks, doors, and identification cards, there is no alert system to indicate unauthorized access to patient information. But there was less chance of stealing the paper record. With the implementation of EMR, if a person steals login information, there is a chance that he may access information which is not related to him.

EMR and its Role in Healthcare Organizations

The main purpose of implementing EMR documentation remains the same — patient care. The clinical documentation is scanned into an electronic system immediately, even before the patient gets discharged. It is possible to view this electronic medical record by many users at the same time, requiring a host of information technology tools.

Patients can review their records regularly, keeping track of their complete diagnoses, healthcare websites, and information about their physician. Now patients can also access their health records with patient logins — patients should be made aware of the danger of sharing access to the login.

The owner of the electronic medical record is the healthcare practice, organization, or physician — as it is the business record. The information, however, always belongs to the patient. This is why security, privacy, confidentiality, availability, and data integrity are the three main ethical priorities which must be considered by an electronic medical record system. Hacking is one other thing that can happen if the EMR is compromised, so practices must select a properly secured EMR.

Ethical Priorities

It is very important that before releasing patient information, patient consent must always be obtained. The patient has state, federal, and legal rights for viewing, getting a copy of, and amending information present in their record.

Staff handling the records should be made aware of the ethical priorities. Today there is a high need for work ethics training, and monitoring has also become essential for all practices to avoid misuse of information.

Access must be limited to authorized individuals with usernames, passwords, and a 2-tier authentication approach — including biometric identifiers like fingerprint, palm, face recognition, or retina scan. Today, HIPAA agreements matter even more since the possibilities of breach have increased multifold.

There are additional concerns as well — electronic data, even though deleted, can be recovered by software designed for that purpose. So if there is a hardware problem, care must be taken when giving computers or hard drives for repair. It is better to have someone come in-house to do it. Furthermore, all faulty hardware which has no scope of repair must be destroyed completely such that the data is also destroyed.

There is always increasing concern about health information security. Earlier, paper records had very limited access — only to doctors and practice staff. With electronic medical records, they can be accessed by anyone with login access to the system. Practices must take this responsibility seriously.

title: The Advent of Medical Scribes
excerpt: From a mutually beneficial arrangement with medical students to a booming remote scribing industry, discover how medical scribes became an essential part of modern physician workflows.
author: Seyyone Team
content:
It started as a mutually beneficial arrangement for the medical student who acted as a scribe and the physician, and now the physicians are looking at scribes as an essential part of their work force. Most of the physicians agree that they hate the EHR/EMR system over the previous transcription system. The main reason for this being the physicians are finding it harder and harder that they have to spend more time and energy filling in the details into EHR/EMR system and the practices are pushing them to do it more promptly and quickly so that the billing goes quick and smooth.

The big loss for the physicians is that they are only able to see 60% to 70% of patients during the allotted time. When the novel idea of medical students acting as scribe to get hands-on experience was developed, it made the physicians think that they can use it as a regular practice which will allow them to see more patients. So they started employing nursing staff or transcriptionists to act as their scribes. The only problem is the cost was very high. The employed staff is highly skilled in medicine and EMRs, so they had to be paid more. But still physicians were willing to do this because it reduced much of their stress and allowed them to concentrate more on patients.

The Rise of Remote Scribing

Finding skilled scribes was becoming more and more difficult in the present scenario because of the high cost and less resources. So the physicians were looking at other options. The only other option available to them was remote scribing. This way, the scribe does not need to be present there physically with the physician. The scribe can just listen to the conversation via some messenger service like Skype, Yahoo over a PC or a mobile and can just enter the details to the EMR from a remote location. This was less costly and it avoided the awkwardness of having somebody else in the room while examining the patient. There are a lot of scribing companies which provide either live physical scribe or remote scribe according to the physician's requirement.

India's Role in Remote Medical Scribing

Since it was possible to do it from a remote location, scribing was experimented from remote countries like India, and it was an immediate success due to the highly skilled and affordable workforce down here. Now most of the practices have begun to opt for remote medical scribes from India and India has been providing the services on a regular basis with her vast experience in medical transcription. Indian transcription industry has been able to adapt to this new development pretty quickly and now it is becoming a more prominent service for most of the transcription companies, beating medical transcription.

The Road Ahead

As of now, only about 10% of physicians are using the remote medical scribing service, and it is expected that it is going to go up to more than 50% in five years' time and India is one country which is going to have the lion's share because of her highly skilled huge work force and affordable cost.

Being from the Medical Transcription background, I have seen firsthand the positive impact of remote medical scribe on the medical practices. Seyyone, the company I work in, is an Indian healthcare BPO/KPO company, specializing in Medical Transcription, EMR-Centric Services, Medical Coding, Medical Billing, etc. It is one such company which has quickly adapted to this new technology and has been rapidly acquiring new clients. When we were thinking that medical transcription is going downhill, the recent emergence of remote medical scribe has renewed the hope for all Transcription companies that this is the new way to go ahead and it looks greatly promising because ultimately the more the physicians/practices are satisfied, the more the hope for the transcription/scribing industry.

title: Seyyone Assisting a Urology Clinic With Their EMR Management and Scribe
excerpt: How Seyyone partnered with a urology clinic since 2014 to streamline EMR management, document filing, lab abstraction, and order confirmations — growing into full coding and billing services.
author: Seyyone Team
content:
In 2014, the top level management of a urology clinic got in contact with Seyyone looking for options whether Seyyone can help them with their EMR management and Scribe. Considering the seriousness involved with the process, the president of the practice, a urology surgeon, came down to Coimbatore to have a discussion with the Seyyone team over this. Our team of highly skilled medical transcriptionists and EMR management team had a series of brainstorming sessions with the physician and we found various ways of helping them out with EMR management.

Filing Scanned Documents

The first thing that was demanding much of their time and resources was the filing of scanned documents into appropriate patient records. This seemingly simple job needed a lot of concentration and time to do it accurately. Any mistake in this could lead to one patient's files ending up in another patient's records, which could create a lot of confusion for the reviewing physician. So, we ensured them that our EMR management team is well qualified, as they have years of medical transcription experience and they will be utmost careful in handling medical records and we proved the same.

Lab Value Abstraction

The second thing was abstraction of most relevant lab values into the patient's EMR. For a urology clinic, urinalysis and PSA are the most important lab values which every physician would like to review whenever a patient is seen at a urology clinic. So our urology clinic had its EMR designed in such a way that it had options to enter these values into the patient's EMR so that it would be easy to compare the recent ones with the older ones. This again was a work which required utmost concentration and accuracy and we could easily help with our EMR management team.

Confirming Physician Orders

The third thing the physician was concerned about was confirmation of physicians' orders getting realized before the patients come in for their follow-up visits. The physician might have ordered some labs, diagnostic studies in the previous visit and the patient may have forgotten to get this lab work done, so the patient needs to be reminded about this, at least two days before the appointment.

What we suggested was that two days before the appointment, we will check the patient's previous visit orders and find out whether the orders have been carried out, whether the results are available, if not, we would inform the clinic's front office staff who would then remind the patient about this. This worked wonders for them and now there are no missing order results whatsoever when the patient checks in.

The Result

After training our team with the above services, the physician spent two more days auditing the work before he gave the nod to go ahead with these services. He was extremely satisfied with our work and we have been successfully providing these services to them since 2014.

Seyyone was able to prove its adaptability with its excellent resources for this urology clinic and sees this as a new area of opportunity to grow into more avenues of EMR management services. Now since Seyyone has successfully accomplished all of the above services, the urology clinic has offered Seyyone coding and billing services too, which Seyyone is equally competently handling to the client's utmost satisfaction.

title: How Indian Healthcare BPO Can Contribute To Macra/Mips in the Future?
excerpt: As MACRA/MIPS reshapes the US healthcare payment system, Indian healthcare BPO companies like Seyyone are uniquely positioned to help physicians navigate the reporting burden cost-effectively.
author: Seyyone Team
content:
Given the elaborate explanations and simplified demonstrations, physicians believed the transition of the current payment system to MACRA/MIPS would be an added advantage going forward. But still many believed this would be causing a biggest overhaul of the system and would require more time to implement and many lobbied to cause a delay in the implementation. Unfortunately it didn't get delayed, but has arrived on time. Physicians and practice managers are still perplexed about the whole process and how much time they should really invest in to carry out the processes involved. Recently, Trump administration has been trying hard to repeal many components of ObamaCare and Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services – CMS has been silent on how it is going to affect MACRA/MIPS process.

The Reality of MACRA/MIPS

The reality is, whether "ObamaCare" or "TrumpCare," it seems that with some modifications, most of the components of MACRA/MIPS are here to stay. With the implementation of EMR, the clinics are already finding it hard to see more patients on a daily basis. The main reasons being doctors having to spend a lot more time before the computer, the complexities involved in chart preparation, and requirement of more front office staff to handle EMR.

It will be even more difficult to concentrate on preparing reports for MACRA/MIPS simultaneously. Even though most of the EMRs have been revised to include the option of reporting MACRA/MIPS components directly, they require the details to be entered into specific areas so that they can get directly reported. The physicians have just settled down with their chart preparation techniques for their respective EMRs. Most of them still do not use drop downs or templates and use freestyle as their format for chart preparation since it is less complex and demands less of their time. So these physicians now again would have to adjust to a new kind of chart creation, keeping in mind reporting for MACRA/MIPS.

The Cost Challenge

This is again going to make them see less patients and spend more of their precious time in mere clerical processes. Another option of reporting MACRA/MIPS as a separate process without using the EMR is widely viable, but this requires employing someone at least as skilled as a nurse, so that the reporting can be done accurately. This again increases overheads for the practices. The cost incurred might actually be more than the incentive by MACRA/MIPS.

Where India Can Help

This is where India can lend a helping hand with her highly skilled medical transcription experience. Since Indians work overnight, they can convincingly report the MACRA/MIPS components the same day, and there will be no pending reports for the next day. The cost to the practices would be greatly reduced, thus they will be able to fully utilize the incentive for reporting in MACRA/MIPS.

We at Seyyone Software Solutions Private Ltd, with more than 17 years experience in medical transcription in India, have been closely observing these happenings in the US and have already offered our assistance to all our existing clients. They are just getting the MACRA/MIPS sorted out and they are waiting for it to settle down. They have informed us they are surely going to look at options to use Seyyone's team for assistance. We, at Seyyone, would make sure to assist the physicians out of this crisis and expect more offers coming our way once MACRA/MIPS has stabilized and the practices start reporting on a regular basis.

title: Dermatologists desperately need scribes – why?
excerpt: Dermatologists are among the busiest specialists, yet EMR adoption has cut their patient load by 50%. Discover why scribes are the perfect solution for dermatology practices.
author: Seyyone Team
content:
Dermatologists are one of the most busiest specialists and I know a few doctors who see more than 80 patients in a day, stretching between 7 am to 5 pm. More than 50% of the patients would require some procedure like liquid nitrogen therapy, injections, biopsy, excision, etc., performed in the clinic. I am amazed at how dermatologists are able to manage all these, they are the real superheroes. They do all these always with a smile on their faces. It is their passion, obviously.

The EMR Impact on Dermatology

Advent of EMR has had significant impact on the time spent and interaction between physician and patient. The doctor has to now concentrate on both the patient and on entering details into the EMR at the same time. The usual smile on the doctor's face has been replaced with a grim and saddening look as he shuttles between the patient and the EMR. Patient looks irritated, and is not sure whether the physician is giving his full attention and care.

Dermatologists, I would say, are the most affected by EMRs, they are not even seeing 50% of the patients they used to see previously. How huge a loss is this, for both the physician and the patient. Also dermatology EMRs are designed in a more complex way, with pictorial representations, and different options to incorporate MACRA/MIPS measures. This has made dermatologists spending more and more time on EMR than any other specialists.

The Numbers Tell the Story

ScenarioTime with Patient (min)Time on EMR (min)Patients/Day
Before EMR5 to 15–80+
After EMR3 to 510 to 1540

The Scribe Solution

Dermatologists were desperate to find a solution. And Bang!, it was right before them. Scribes. But where live scribes tagging along can be a great asset to ER physicians, is it the same for a dermatologist? I don't think so. Patients with skin conditions can become quite apprehensive during physical examination and subsequent discussions when a scribe is tagging along. So live scribe is out of the question. The only solution left was using scribes who can enter the charts into the EMR overnight. The only thing the doctor had to do was dictate and send audios to the scribe, some doctors even prefer to send rough transcripts using a voice recognition software like Dragon.

Dermatologists once again started seeing more than 80 patients in a day with a constant smile on their faces. Thanks to scribes, dermatologists and their patients experience extreme satisfaction in the encounters, not worrying about EMR. I work for a medical scribe services company, Seyyone Software Solutions, India, which has been in existence for nearly two decades. I have had hands-on experience scribing for one such dermatologist from Massachusetts. Now the dermatologist is extremely happy with the scribing experience and is expanding his clinic by adding 6 to 7 dermatologists to the practice.

It is my humble opinion that dermatologists, more than any other specialists, desperately need a scribe's help. Wouldn't they want to avoid losing 50% revenue per month by just spending about $2000–$3000 per month for a scribe?

title: Skilled Data Entry, An Extra Burden On Medical Assistants?!
excerpt: EMR implementation has placed a heavy data entry burden on medical assistants. Could outsourcing skilled data entry be the solution doctors need to see more patients?
author: Seyyone Team
content:
Implementation of EMRs has necessitated a lot of data entry like lab values, medications, allergies, family history, social history, etc. Previously, before EMRs, if the physician wanted to see the chronological difference in a lab value, he would just take the printed sheets of the lab values of different dates and look through them.

EMRs have made it easy for the physician to check the chronological difference by providing options to enter lab values such that, the physician can just look at the list of lab values in the order of their dates and observe the difference in a single click. What took them 3 to 5 minutes previously, now takes them less than a minute to just check it out. Other data like medications, allergies also are now meticulously updated into the EMR such that the doctor can check the recent updates in a single click.

The Data Entry Challenge

If a new patient comes to the clinic, the doctors, along with updating the visit note, have to enter the family history, social history, past medical history, which they get from the patient's previous medical records. Now with EMRs, the doctors have the option of getting somebody else to update these and keep the note ready for them to just enter the chief complaint, history of present illness, review of systems, physical examination, and assessment and plan, which are usually done by the doctor. This saves them a lot of time.

All these data entry requires knowledge in medicine and only medical assistants can do these and that is why the term skilled data entry. Since the implementation of EMRs, most of the medical assistants spend more than 80% of their time in front of the computers doing data entry.

The Impact on Patient Care

Doctors are already facing delays and are seeing less number of patients because of EMRs and now without the much needed help of medical assistants, it is getting even more difficult to see patients as per the schedule. A few doctors have started thinking about utilizing the medical transcription industry for these skilled data entry works so that they can utilize their medical assistants more effectively to see more patients.

Seyyone Software Solutions is one such company with nearly two decades of experience in medical transcription which has fortunately pioneered in such data entry works and has been doing it for most of its clients to their utmost satisfaction. Hopefully, more and more doctors would seek to outsource such data entry works so that they may overcome the difficulties they face with EMR and get back to their original form and serve more patients, which is and always have been their ultimate goal.

title: How outsourcing back office services help medical practices
excerpt: From cost savings and improved efficiency to regulatory compliance and better patient care — discover how outsourcing back office services gives medical practices a competitive edge.
author: Seyyone Team
content:
Outsourcing back office services can provide a wide range of benefits for medical practices. The healthcare industry is rapidly changing, and medical practices need to keep up with the latest technology, regulations, and patient demands. Outsourcing can help medical practices achieve these goals and improve their overall efficiency and profitability.

Cost Savings

One of the major benefits of outsourcing back office services is cost savings. Medical practices can save a significant amount of money by outsourcing certain tasks that can be performed at a lower cost in another country — including administrative support, data entry, billing, and appointment scheduling. In addition, outsourcing helps reduce overhead costs by eliminating the need to hire and train new employees.

Increased Efficiency

Medical practices often struggle to manage a large volume of tasks, especially during peak periods. Outsourcing can help manage this workload more efficiently, allowing practices to focus on their core competencies such as patient care and medical procedures. Additionally, outsourcing provides access to the latest technology and software, improving overall productivity and accuracy.

Access to Expertise

An outsourcing company that specializes in back-office services will have a team of highly trained professionals who are knowledgeable about the latest technologies, regulations, and best practices in the industry. This can help medical practices stay compliant and competitive in a rapidly changing healthcare landscape.

Improved Data Management

Outsourcing companies often use advanced technology and systems to manage data, reducing the risk of errors and increasing the accuracy of records. This improved data management helps medical practices meet regulatory requirements, improve patient care, and support informed decision-making.

Focus on Core Competencies

By outsourcing back office services, medical practices can focus on delivering quality patient care. This helps practices maintain a high level of patient satisfaction, improve clinical outcomes, and boost their reputation in the community.

Regulatory Compliance

Medical practices are subject to a number of regulations and guidelines such as HIPAA and the Affordable Care Act, and non-compliance can result in hefty fines. By outsourcing certain tasks like patient data management, medical practices can ensure their operations remain in compliance with all relevant regulations.

In conclusion, outsourcing back office services provides significant benefits — more time, flexibility, cost savings, and access to expertise. By partnering with a reliable outsourcing company like Seyyone, practices can improve efficiency, improve data management, and focus on their core competency of delivering quality patient care. Outsourcing is an attractive option for medical practices looking to stay competitive and efficient in today's healthcare landscape.

title: All about outsourcing virtual office assistance for office managers
excerpt: From filing medical records to entering demographics into EMR — virtual office assistants are helping practice managers reclaim their time and grow their business at a fraction of the cost.
author: Seyyone Team
content:
Virtual office assistants can be the best choice for many businesses and industries. When it comes to practice managers, a virtual office assistant can really help and take care of most administrative tasks. Especially for a growing practice, a virtual office assistant can provide the efficient work needed.

There are many solo practitioners who struggle to handle entire work by themselves. Especially when the waiting list grows, it is the time to think about bringing assistance in. For both solo practitioners and healthcare sectors, there are many tasks to handle on a daily basis — filing medical records, printing medication lists, faxing notes to referring physicians, entering demographics into EMR, etc. These tasks, even though they look simple, eat up a lot of time. When someone else takes care of these tasks, physicians are able to spend the saved time focusing on providing sessions and other creative things which help in growing the business.

Benefits of Virtual Assistant

When tasks are delegated, it results in efficient use of time and effort. Delegating tasks is one among the most important management principles in any industry. In case a physician starts doing all the office or administrative tasks, he will not get time for patients.

Hiring an office assistant as an employee in the office can cost a lot, making it an expensive option. This is the reason most practitioners today prefer virtual assistants — they can be located anywhere and are less expensive. Once the virtual assistant setup is done, it can be as seamless as possible.

Finding the Right Person

When it comes to finding the virtual assistant, they should be trustworthy and reliable. The person hired should be skilled in medical data management and should be professional. Knowledge of medical practice and therapy would be an added advantage. Virtual assistants should be organized, capable of working effectively, and proactive at their work.

Some practitioners suggest hiring a person you already know. But if you cannot find one, there are a lot of professional virtual assistant services through which it is possible to hire a professional virtual assistant.

Final Thoughts

Today there are many cloud-based and virtual tools available. It is better to choose the ones which are most commonly used and free. Some practitioners make use of their Gmail account and Google Voice for virtual assistant setup. HIPAA regulations need to be kept in mind when allowing someone to manage data on EHR. Professional healthcare KPO service providers who have been in the industry for a long time with excellent HIPAA credentials would be the right choice.

Since there are many tools and technologies available to support virtual office assistants, it is the better choice compared to hiring a physical assistant. Hiring a virtual assistant is less expensive, and along with saving money, it is possible to get the right talent to take care of most administrative tasks that need manual engagement — especially with EMR.

title: Revolution of virtual scribing with smartphone recording apps
excerpt: Smartphone recording apps are transforming virtual scribing, enabling real-time EMR documentation while freeing physicians to focus entirely on their patients.
author: Seyyone Team
content:
Virtual scribing is one among the revolutionary developments we have seen in the healthcare sector. Virtual scribing has enabled systems to securely manage their patient data in the EMR in real-time. A virtual medical scribe is a professional who has the capability to listen to the discussion which happens between patient and doctor, and interprets it into meaningful patient health data. Today there are many smartphone recording apps which are taking a major role in revolutionizing virtual scribes.

How it Works

When a physician makes use of a scribe, the doctor can freely consult the patient without having to worry about preparing charts and making notes. This is because the scribe is there to work on charting the patient's health record in real-time. This setup needs high quality microphones in the exam rooms along with provision of the scribe accessing the EMR.

The scribe will document the entire chart including history of the current illness, surgical history, past medical history, social and family history, physical examination, review of systems, labs and imaging, procedure and diagnosis coding, and E&M and plan coding. These things will be documented within a few minutes during the doctor-patient discussion. Soon after the discussion the physician can review the chart and send it for billing.

Smartphone Recording Apps and Virtual Scribes

Today there are many high quality recording apps available on smartphones which are really helping virtual scribes. The virtual scribe will not be present in the examination room, but will be able to listen and chart the discussion between doctor and patient. The scribe will also enter all the notes, CPT or ICD codes, along with all the other relevant data like eRx, and the patient chart is created.

Later the doctor will get the opportunity to review the chart with the virtual scribe via a secured VOIP connection. If needed, edits will be made to the patient's chart and the doctor will sign off.

In case of a normal physical scribe, the scribe must be physically present in the exam room. This process can be comparatively expensive. More than that, the majority of patients feel discomfort when a person other than the doctor is present in the exam room. The main drawback of a physical scribe is that when there is demand, it is difficult to move them from one location to another without loss of time. If the physical scribe is sick or on leave, someone else must jump in and complete the work.

Final Thoughts

Earlier, physicians were spending most of their time updating medical records in computers, leaving less time for examination. Even after work hours, they were spending a lot of time completing charts and other documentation. This is where virtual scribes come in to lessen the physician's burden, allowing doctors to concentrate only on examining patients.

With the revolution in technology, there are many apps and platforms which can be used for virtual scribes. Among them, the most useful are recording apps available on smartphones. These recording apps have revolutionized virtual scribes — documentation and chart preparation have become easier, simpler, and more accurate.

title: Limitations of Voice Recognition Technology in EMRs
excerpt: Voice recognition software in EMRs promises to save time but is error-prone. A JAMA study reveals how documentation errors and insufficient clinical review are creating new burdens for physicians.
author: Seyyone Team
content:
There are a few studies which have recently shown that voice recognition software which generates EMR clinical documentation is error-prone. One of these studies is the JAMA study, which was focused mainly on identifying and analyzing the mistakes at every stage of the voice recognition process. The main aim was to provide the right recommendations for ensuring that EMR documentation maintains a high level of accuracy.

Today there is a high rate of adoption of speech recognition technology in most healthcare institutions, mainly because documentation in the healthcare system is the most time-consuming job even today. Voice recognition systems aim to improve the efficiency of documentation and save a lot of time for clinicians.

After review by a transcriptionist, there was a 0.4% decrease in error rate, and 0.3% was the decreased error rate in the final version of EMR documentation reviewed and signed by clinicians. Even though error rates were comparatively reduced after clinical review, it demanded investigation by clinicians — creating an administrative burden after the implementation of voice recognition in EMRs.

The aim was to reduce documentation time pressure on clinicians and make them review notes superficially. When the responsibility shifted from transcriptionists to clinicians, this led to enhanced documentation errors since, in many cases, clinicians were not able to review notes correctly.

Front End vs Back End Implementation

Healthcare organizations can implement speech recognition both at the front end and back end. Back-end systems automatically convert clinician dictations into text, after which medical transcriptionists quickly review and edit the clinical documentation for accuracy.

When the responsibility of reviewing notes is transferred to medical transcriptionists, this frees up physicians to focus on patient care, boosting clinical productivity while reducing administrative burden. When implemented at the front end, clinicians must edit their notes themselves after reviewing — which enhances administrative burden.

Even though the main focus of voice recognition in EMR was to ease documentation burden, there is no clarity on time available for physicians to review dictated notes. Inaccuracies happen when there is insufficient review of the final version. When clinically relevant information is affected by documentation errors, this may seriously pose a threat to patient safety.

Final Thoughts

Timely and accurate documentation is essential for patient care, accurate billing, and all insurance and legal purposes. Speech recognition software has seen an adoption increase from 21% to 47%. Majority of clinicians believe it helps improve workflow — it allows automatic queuing of dictations, predefined assistance, and selection routing of dictation files, reducing turnaround time.

But almost all tools can make mistakes, especially in noisy environments. Physicians face issues like overused punctuation, missing punctuation, poor grammar, and disorganized dictations. Software also struggles with heavy accents and unique dictation styles, requiring human intervention for training and pattern recognition.

So it is observed that human intervention is still necessary, and the need for transcriptionists still prevails to a huge extent. Voice recognition software is a powerful tool, but it works best when paired with skilled human review.

title: Generate Maximum Revenue – A Know-How on Chiropractic Billing
excerpt: More patients does not always mean more revenue. Discover the key billing strategies every chiropractic clinic needs to reduce denials, improve cash flow, and generate maximum revenue.
author: Seyyone Team
content:
Every clinic that we see these days is busy on its average day. When you have a glimpse at a chiropractic clinic, it may top the chart each time. Amidst these more than average number of patients, your staff might experience a burn-out situation. A major misconception is that as more patients start to visit, the clinic is loaded with revenue. In reality, their documentation, coding, billing, federal and insurance program requirements, and pending account receivables make it even more difficult to produce an average revenue for a chiropractor. The practice has to start handling their office management and billing on the same scale in order to create an effective revenue cycle.

Common Problems Every Chiropractic Clinic Faces

  • Documentation does not always sufficiently comply with insurance company and federal requirements
  • The patient's plan does not have coverage benefits for the services offered by the provider
  • Poor coding and inappropriate billing — coding errors make a frequent ground for denial
  • Each work is assigned to an individual with a one-task-at-a-time work strategy; staff unable to cope with changing provisions
  • Every day struggle with underpayment or no-payment

Tips for Generating Maximum Revenue

Detailed Documentation

Claims require sufficient documentation when the adjudication department demands additional information. When a claim is denied for medical necessity or additional information, focus primarily on producing precise documentation. This helps the biller choose the procedures and diagnosis appropriately on the claim while complying with LCD guidelines.

Advanced Pre-determination

Get all the required patient demographics and insurance information upfront. For established chiropractic patients coming regularly for follow-up visits, update their information periodically. For new patients, verify coverage benefits, type and number of visits covered. Most commercial insurances ask for prior authorization, and some help providers obtain retro authorization within 72 hours of the date of service.

Examine Your Denial Trends

A major factor affecting clinic revenue is denied claims. We have observed a maximum of one denial for every four claims submitted and a minimum of one denial for every ten claims submitted. List out common denials like coding errors, name mismatch, prior authorization, out of network, and medical necessity. Assign these to separate buckets, analyze the pattern periodically, and create necessary updates to avoid these denial trends in the future.

Evaluate Your Receivables

Filter your aging by insurances and their ominous payments, then divide the aging report by every insurance's timely filing limit. Create a follow-up protocol for every eight days. Our target is to make the average turnaround time for payment 25–40 days. Track and prepare reports on claims and aging to find under-billing and underpaid claims.

The Optimum Billing

Always focus on sending clean claims at a rate of 95%. Your first submission claim should be scrubbed enough to prevent any common denials. After charge entry, auditing takes some more time before releasing the claim — but it is worth it. It is always better to wait a few minutes before sending the claim than spending hours fixing it. A well-documented record helps the coder choose accurate codes.

All these tips are only a few strands on the fine-weaved package. Practices can also take advantage of coding software, temporary consultants, and virtual vendors to streamline billing. A transparent bottom line is to produce revenue and stay consistent about it.

title: Scope and Growth of Artificial Intelligence in American Healthcare Industry
excerpt: From brain-computer interfaces to AI-powered radiology tools and smart ICU devices — explore how artificial intelligence is transforming every corner of the American healthcare industry.
author: Seyyone Team
content:
Healthcare industry in America is ripe enough for accepting significant changes. From severe diseases like cancer, treatments like radiology, and even in risk management, there is an infinite number of opportunities for leveraging technologies. Through this, it is possible to deploy efficient, precise, and highly impactful intervention at exactly the right time in patient care.

As payment structures evolve, patients are demanding more and more facilities from providers. Along with this, the volume of data that should be handled by providers is increasing tremendously. Artificial intelligence can be the right thing to lead a revolution in the healthcare industry.

AI can bring in a lot of advantages compared to traditional techniques used for clinical decision making and analytics. Learning algorithms become more and more accurate as they interact with training data. With the help of AI, humans can gain specific insights on patient outcomes, treatment variability, care processes, and diagnostics.

Brain Computing Interfaces – Unifying Machine and Mind

The original idea here is creating direct interfaces between the human mind and technology, without any media like mice, keyboards, or monitors. This is a cutting-edge research area with important applications for patients suffering from neurological diseases or trauma to the human nervous system that results in loss of ability to move and speak.

BCI or Brain-Computer Interface, backed by AI, can restore some of these lost experiences for patients. This is mainly helpful for patients suffering from strokes, ALS, locked-in syndrome, and spinal cord injuries.

EHR and AI

EHRs have already played an instrumental role in healthcare digitalization. But EHR brought a myriad of problems — user burnout, endless documentation, and cognitive overload. EHR developers have now started moving towards AI to create more intuitive interfaces and automate routine processes.

Through AI, voice recognition and dictation can improve the clinical documentation process. AI can also help in processing routine requests such as result and refill notifications, and prioritizing tasks that were previously taking clinicians' attention.

Radiology Tools

Radiology images obtained by CT scanners, MRI machines, and x-ray offer high-level visibility to the human body. But many diagnostic processes still rely on physical tissue samples from biopsies, which carry high risks such as contamination.

Through AI, it is possible to enable next-generation radiology tools that are detailed and accurate enough to replace the necessity of tissue samples. Technologies like virtual biopsies are making significant advancements in the radiomics field, helping providers define cancer aggressiveness and target treatments more appropriately.

Expanding Care into Developing Regions

There is significantly limited access to life-saving care in underdeveloped and developing nations due to shortages in healthcare providers like radiologists and ultrasound technicians. AI can take over diagnostic duties allocated to humans — for example, using x-ray to screen tuberculosis with a high level of accuracy, deployed via an app in low-resourced areas, reducing the need for trained radiologists.

Pathology Images and Precise Analytics

Analytics can be drilled down to pixel level on large digital photographs, finding nuances that otherwise escape human findings. Through AI, it is possible to improve productivity by identifying features of interest in a slide before clinicians even review the data.

Medical Devices and Machines – Bringing Intelligence

In the medical environment, smart devices can help in monitoring critical conditions like patients in ICU. Through AI, it is possible to identify deterioration early and suggest ways to avoid complications.

Final Thoughts

On the whole, AI can be used in all areas of healthcare management — whether it is billing management, prescription management, or EHR. Through this implementation, it is possible to reduce the number of staff needed, achieve accurate documentation, and quicken the process of claims. But it is also essential that AI should be appropriately handled and implementation given to the right hands.

title: Learn Effective ways to surpass Patient Restrictions and Staff troubles at your Clinic
excerpt: From staff burnout and patient dissatisfaction to physician credentialing and insurance enrollment — here are proven strategies every practice administrator needs to tackle clinic challenges head-on.
author: Seyyone Team
content:
Like every other blog, to get started, it has to talk about your pain points. There are pain points which every practice administrator faces, and they always wish that these could have been solved earlier.

  • Every employee at your practice will be assigned for a specific task. But the issue arises when we come across people who say it's not part of their job description.
  • Staffs fear their growth in this industry. They get worried that they are not being effectively engaged to develop their potential.
  • Everyone demands time off their work. When their hours are covered by another staff, a lot of workload and pressure is involved. You will start seeing many patients leaving your office dissatisfied, complaining they are not receiving enough attention.
  • It is tough to find people who are highly skilled enough to help you meet patient care. Recruiting physicians and their respective medical staffs has become a major issue in small and medium level practices.
  • One after another, these issues end up with losing patients that arrive at your office.

As the administrator of your practice, when you think of insurance, there comes the concept of enrolling your physician and your practice into an insurance panel. The enrollment process involves two steps: Credentialing and Contracting.

In credentialing, it involves the verification of your credentials and supporting documentation. Once approved, a contractual agreement is sent to the physician who must agree with the standard fee schedule or reimbursement rates. In some cases, you can negotiate these terms with the insurance.

Once a physician is recruited, they must be credentialed with key insurances in their county, otherwise it may result in:

  • An increased chance of denials on the basis of out-of-network as well as prior authorization
  • Delay in cash flow
  • Patient restrictions and scheduling difficulties
  • Relentless engagement with payers even for clean claims

Solutions

  1. Cross-train every staff on multiple aspects of your office. It cannot prevent burnout, but it will help staff take care of works aside from their job description.
  2. Multi-department training helps staff develop their potential and saves you from over-recruiting.
  3. Recruit temps or virtual vendors to cover staff time-off for front and back-office work so medical staff can focus on patient care. The happier the patient, the more likely they are to return and refer friends.
  4. Address staff recruitment challenges by offering flexibility in office hours, time-off, and better pay. Once you recruit a qualified physician or nurse practitioner, the next challenge is insurance credentialing.

Quick Notes for Physician Credentialing

Start with Credentials

Verify provider information such as Education and Employment history, Professional IDs like Board Certification Number, TAX ID, Taxonomy, Medical license, NPI, DEA and CDS registrations, and Professional Liability insurance. Also verify Practice location, Mailing Address, and professional references.

Understanding the Regulations

Every state may have their own laws and so do the insurances. Some insurances have their own clauses in recruiting a physician to their network, including limits on how many physicians they accept. This will hurt every new physician's pocket who is trying to get into their network.

CAQH and Commercial Insurances

A well-crafted CAQH profile would always be a PLUS while enrolling physicians in an insurance network. It is a more adopted program by today's commercial payers. Every provider's credentials information shall be maintained and shared with affiliated payers on demand.

A Prodigal Application

Make sure the application surpasses the process the first time after receiving all the credentials. There should not be any repetitions in acquiring information from the provider, as this may lead to more chances of rework.

The Right Person for the Job

It is essential to employ a Credentialing Specialist or Coordinator to manage the process. Either in-house or out of the office, this helps the process to be monitored, communicated, and followed up on.

Employing someone in-house specifically for credentialing demands hourly pay, taxes, employee benefits, 401K, and paid leaves. When you employ an outside company, you get to utilize them on a need-of-the-hour basis. A dedicated team focusing on a specific task with uninterrupted follow-ups is far more efficient — and the physician always knows where his enrollment stands.

title: Evolution Of Medical Transcription Into Scribing - 2
excerpt: From HIPAA to MACRA/MIPS, discover how EMR mandates transformed medical transcription and gave birth to the scribing profession as we know it today.
author: Seyyone Team
content:
Although the use of electronic medical records (EMRs) had been in existence since 1960s, not much attention was given to it until the passing of Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) in 1996. This act stressed upon more emphasis on high-level security and confidentiality while handling medical records and also prescribed penalties for those who did not follow these regulations. Many big organizations started to move towards EMRs to ensure compliance of HIPAA regulations.

Since computers and Internet were getting much cheaper and becoming easily available with user-friendly technologies during the 2000s, American Federal Government started to think about implementing 100% EMR policy, and President Bush's budget called for an industry-wide adoption of EMRs by 2014. This mandate was also supported by President Obama and incentives were suggested to practitioners who adopt EMRs and follow the concept of meaningful use by 2014. Beginning in 2015, penalties were implemented on practitioners who had not yet adopted EMRs.

The Impact of EMR Adoption

Both the fear of penalties and the lure of incentives led physicians to rapidly adopt EMRs. Adoption of EMRs brought about a big change in medical transcription, as all the notes now needed to be typed directly into the EMR. For the doctors, it didn't make their life easier, it made it worse by demanding much of their time before EMR. The doctors slowly got drawn into doing more data entry work than caring for patients.

They were seeing lesser and lesser patients per day, incurring heavy financial losses and were getting burnt out due to much time spent on their computers. Many medical transcription companies sensed this change early on and made themselves available for training on EMRs and were ready to provide the transcription services via EMR. Doctors who were outsourcing their transcription work previously now once again started using the transcription services to work on EMR.

Doctors began to choose EMRs which were far simpler to create notes, like having the option of typing the whole note into a single page in the EMR. They usually ignored the use of templates, drop down menus as it took more time and the end result was not up to their satisfaction.

The MACRA/MIPS Challenge

Yet another challenge was awaiting, which made the doctors rethink about the options of creating notes in EMR; the introduction of MACRA/MIPS. The introduction of MACRA/MIPS, an advanced form of meaningful use, implemented from 2017, requires doctors to report quality measures and ensure proper use of EMR. Improper reporting or no reporting would lead to penalties and lesser payments from Medicare.

To make the reporting easy, most of the EMRs started to incorporate technology to abstract information from the notes created by doctors. So doctors now face the challenge of creating notes keeping in mind the implementation of MACRA/MIPS measures. This once again demands much of the doctors' time and reduces their ability to see more number of patients. Now medical transcription companies are gearing up to this new challenge and are rigorously training themselves to support the doctors.

How the Transcriptionist Turned Into a Scribe

When doctors were training medical students in their practice, they used the students to create notes on EMR while they cared for patients. This slowly developed into a regular practice. Both doctors and medical students found it mutually beneficial, the doctors having found a solution for their transcription needs and the students had an opportunity to learn more.

What started as a learning experiment slowly became a part-time job opportunity for medical students and they began to be called scribes. Since the students had to leave and start their own practices, the doctors who had gotten used to having scribes now desperately needed scribes and they started looking at other healthcare-related staff, like nurses, medical assistants who can work as a scribe. Thus slowly and surely scribing became a true profession.
